---
title: "vellumverse #8: Inside the compile pass: from spec to scene"
author:
- name: David Schoch
  orcid: 0000-0003-2952-4812
date: '2026-07-30'
categories:
- R
- data visualization
- vellumverse
draft: true
---

Last post I said `vellumplot` builds a spec and a compiler turns it into a scene.
This post opens the compiler. It is not a metaphor: printing a plot runs a
genuine pipeline of stages, each consuming the output of the last, and the
grammar's nicest properties fall out of the order they run in.

Here is a plot with two layers and a mapped colour.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(vellumplot)
p <- vplot(mtcars) |>
  mark_point(x = wt, y = mpg, color = hp) |>
  mark_smooth(x = wt, y = mpg)
```
:::


When `p` renders, the compiler runs six stages, in this order:

1. Resolve encodings. Evaluate the tidy-eval expressions (`x = wt`, `color = hp`)
   against each layer's data, producing concrete columns of numbers and factors.
2. Apply stats and positions. Marks with a statistical transform compute their
   derived data here (the smooth fits its model), and position adjustments like
   stack, dodge, and jitter are applied.
3. Train scales. Scan the resolved values across every layer to find each scale's
   domain, then build the mapping from data to colour, size, shape, and position.
4. Measure layout. Lay out the panel, or the grid of facet panels, reserving room
   for axes, strips, legends, and titles, and work out where everything lands.
5. Compile guides. Emit the axes, gridlines, and legends the trained scales imply.
6. Compile marks. Turn each layer into `vellum` grobs, positioned in the panel.

Out the other end is a `vellum` scene: the retained, read-back tree from the
earlier posts. The backend renders it to PNG, SVG, or PDF, and `vellumplot` is
done.

The order is not incidental. Three of the grammar's best properties are just
consequences of it.

Scales train after every layer's data is resolved, at stage 3, over all layers at
once. That is the whole reason the point cloud and the fitted line in `p` land on
one set of axes without you aligning anything. There is no drawing order to get
wrong, because nothing has drawn yet when the domain is computed.

Faceting is a layout question rather than a redraw. Splitting into panels happens
at stage 4, over a spec whose data and scales are already resolved, so
`facet_wrap()` reuses the same trained scales across every panel (or trains them
free per panel if you ask) in a single compile. It does not build N plots and
stitch them together.

And because the whole thing is a function from spec to scene, you can look before
you leap. `summary(p)` reported the structure a post ago without drawing anything.
The compiler is what stands behind that promise: the spec genuinely contains the
answer, and compilation is the pure computation that reveals it.

If you want to read the real thing, it is not hidden. The pipeline lives in
`R/seam.R` and the `R/compile-*.R` files inside `vellumplot`, roughly one file
per stage and named about how you would guess (`compile-train.R`,
`compile-layout.R`, `compile-guides.R`, `compile-marks.R`). The prose version is
the [compiler article](https://r-vellum.github.io/vellumplot/articles/the-compiler.html);
the code version is a good afternoon's read.

The next post steps back from the machinery to the surface you actually touch:
the handful of marks and scales it takes to make real plots. After that comes the
payoff act, where the scene this compiler produces becomes a raster, a vector, or
an interactive widget from one spec.
